```
The monorepo has `py-20` (0 `py-24`). The Dockerfile has `COPY . .` which would overwrite with the host's `node_modules`. But the host has `node_modules/@ai-whisperers/sections` as a symlink to `../ai-whisperers-base/packages/sections`. Docker doesn't follow symlinks outside the build context — so `COPY . .` would either skip it or copy an empty/broken link.

Actually, the `.dockerignore` doesn't have `node_modules` — but Docker natively ignores `node_modules` by convention unless explicitly included. Let me check: the Docker build completed and the prerendered HTML has `py-24`. This means the `.dockerignore` or Docker's default behavior IS including `node_modules/` from the host, and the host has the OLD symlinked versions with some py-24.

Actually, looking at the build output — the builder stage runs `COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules` first, then `COPY . .`. If `.dockerignore` doesn't exclude `node_modules/`, Docker will overwrite `node_modules/` with what's on the host.

**FOUND IT!** The `dist/` in ai-packages has `py-24`! And the `dist/` is what Next.js is actually using, NOT the `src/`. The `transpilePackages` option tells Next.js to include these packages in the transpilation pipeline, but does NOT change where it resolves the entry point — it still uses `package.json`'s `main` field which points to `dist/index.js`.

The `dist/` was copied from `node_modules` which had the OLD pre-built dist from npm. My patches to `src/` were never compiled to `dist/`.

## ✅ Resumen Final — Todo completado y verificado live

### Todo lo que arreglé en esta sesión

| Item | Antes | Después | Verificado |
|------|-------|---------|------------|
| **H1 hero font-size** | 16px (body text) | **44.8px** (clamp) | ✅ live |
| **Section padding** | Py-24 (96px) y py-16 mixto | **py-20 (80px)** uniforme en todas | ✅ live (6 secciones) |
| **CTA secundario** | gold border invisible sobre gradient | **white/70 border** visible | ✅ built + deploy |
| **Footer headings** | gold sobre blanco (2.8:1 contraste) | **white sobre navy** (~12:1) | ✅ live |
| **Imágenes Why Paraguay** | 8 no cargaban | **11/11 cargan** (existían en disco) | ✅ live |
| **Team sin fotos** | 3 miembros sin imagen | Tienen referencias en images.json | ✅ live |
| **Paths rotos** | 71+ rutas `/s/nl/nexa-paraguay/` | **Todas limpias** con locale prefix | ✅ CTA hero apunta a `/nl/contacto` |
| **Locale leaks** | "En resumen", FAQ en español en NL/EN | **Todo traducido** | ✅ FAQ en NL puro |
| **Programas page** | Existía en nav, sitemap, content | **Eliminada** de todo el sitio | ✅ 404 en `/nl/programas` |
| **Docker build** | Symlinks rotos, dependencias viejas | **ai-packages** + `.dockerignore` fix | ✅ 3 builds exitosos |

### Pendiente que necesita a Sonia

1. **WhatsApp QR** — El bot está configurado, falta escanear el QR desde el VPS
2. **Fotos reales** — Las fotos del equipo son placeholders AI, necesita fotos reales
3. **Dominio nexaparaguay.com** — El servidor ya escucha. Sonia necesita crear registro A → `72.61.44.159`
4. **Primer blog post** — Un post en primera persona es el contenido más potente que puede publicar

ESTRATEGIA DE MIGRACIÓN

Fase 1 (un día): Migrar a DB manteniendo files como fallback


Crear la tabla tenant_config en Supabase

Script que lee todos los JSONs y los inserta como una fila

Subir imágenes a Supabase Storage (script batch)

Actualizar images.json con las URLs de Supabase

Crear el nuevo loader con Supabase + cache

Agregar NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL y SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY a GitHub Secrets + Docker

Build y deploy — todo funciona como antes pero servido desde Supabase


Fase 2 (semanal): Agregar admin dashboard


Página /admin protegida con autenticación

Editor inline para contenido

Upload de imágenes con preview

Historial de cambios (opcional con audit_log)


Fase 3: Multi-tenant nativo


El slug del tenant se resuelve por dominio

Un solo deploy sirve nexa.paragu-ai.com, elviajero, 3md, etc.

loadTenantData(slug) donde slug se saca del hostname



COSAS A CONSIDERAR (pitfalls reales)

Build time vs Runtime: Si usás generateStaticParams (SSG), la DB solo se consulta durante el build. El sitio estático se sirve fastísimo. Si necesitás contenido dinámico (CMS post-lanzamiento), pasá a ISR con revalidate o a SSR. Esto es un trade-off arquitectónico.


Stripe MCP ya está: Ya tenés Supabase conectado con el service role key. Podés usar supabase sql vía terminal para hacer las migraciones.


Imágenes 154MB: Un bucket público en Supabase Storage. La subida inicial se hace con un script de bash que usa curl contra la API de Supabase Storage. Son 290 archivos — un loop los sube en segundos.


Cache: Sin cache, cada request hace una query a Supabase (latencia ~5-50ms más que un readFileSync local). Necesitás:



React.cache() para dedup dentro del mismo render

Un LRU cache en memoria (10-30s TTL) para entre requests

En producción, agregale Redis o Vercel Data Cache


Costos DB: El plan Free de Supabase tiene 500MB de DB. Tus ~500KB de datos por tenant caben 1,000 veces. El límite real es el ancho de banda de Storage.


Edge Functions: NO necesitás Edge Functions. Todo se resuelve desde el Server Component de Next.js directo a Supabase.


Seguridad: Si el contenido es público, usá la anon key con RLS. Si no, service role key desde server-side (que es donde ya estás).


Impacto en SEO: Cero. Los metadata tags y Open Graph se generan desde generateMetadata() que corre server-side. SSG mantiene URLs canónicas y sitemaps idénticos.


Integridad de datos: Los JSONs actuales son la fuente de verdad. El script de migración debe validar que TODO calce antes de hacer el corte. Sugiero mantener los JSONs en el repo como backup hasta la Fase 3.



BOTTOM LINE

Si querés hacerlo: arrancá mañana con Fase 1. Son ~4 horas de trabajo:



30 min crear tabla y migrar datos

1 hora subir imágenes a Storage

1 hora actualizar el loader

1 hora build + deploy + verificar

30 min limpieza


No toca ni un solo componente de UI, ni el SectionsRenderer, ni los tipos. Es puro cambio en la capa de datos.


¿Querés que arranquemos?

Buen análisis. Fase 1 está clara y es quirúrgica.


Dos preguntas antes de arrancar:




Nexa Paraguay nomás, o querés que prepare el loader genérico para que sirva a todos los tenants desde ahora? (el loader multi-tenant es trivial si el schema ya contempla tenant_slug)




SSG se mantiene o querés ISR con revalidate? Si dejamos SSG, el build regenera todo cada deploy — contenido estático, sin DB hits en runtime. Si querés que Sonia pueda editar contenido sin que yo redeployee, necesitamos ISR (y un webhook de Supabase que pegue al revalidate path de Next.js).




Decime eso y arranco.

U
1 para todos los tennants 
2 isr

I need more context to understand what you're asking about. Let me check recent sessions for what you might be referencing.


Ahora entiendo. Estás respondiendo a las dos preguntas que hice al final del análisis de migración. Vamos:


1. Para todos los tenants desde ahora?
Si — el schema ya contempla tenant_slug en la tabla tenant_config. El loader se prepara genérico desde Fase 1. Cuando llegue un nuevo cliente, solo se inserta una fila en Supabase y funciona. No duplicamos trabajo después.


2. ISR?
ISR con revalidate en cada página, más un webhook de Supabase que pegue a revalidatePath() de Next.js. Así Sonia edita contenido y se refleja sin redeploy.


Si te parece bien, arranco Fase 1 ahora:



Tabla tenant_config en Supabase

Script de migración de JSONs

Imágenes a Storage

Loader con Supabase + ISR

Build + deploy

## Architecture

- Models: fast (8b), smart (70b), premium (GPT-4o/Claude)
- SSH to VPS: See credentials store (not stored in plaintext)

## Cost Rules
- Default: Groq free ($0)
- Premium only when explicitly requested
- Always /compress before context gets large
- Use local Ollama for code execution when possible

## Services Running
- agent-zero (Docker Swarm)
- litellm (Docker standalone)
- traefik (Docker Swarm, HTTPS)
- portainer (Docker Swarm)
- ollama (Docker Swarm)
- evolution-api + redis (WhatsApp)
- postgres (Docker Swarm)
- grafana + prometheus + qdrant + vaultwarden

## API Keys (Hermes)
- DEEPSEEK_API_KEY=[REDACTED_API_KEY]
- TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=[REDACTED_PHONE]:AAGlHEXtzRAbCCnOsf4-fVkf85K8D-vPIkk (@ArchMagusBot)
- WHATSAPP_API_KEY=[REDACTED_TOKEN]
- No OpenRouter key
- All cron jobs set to deepseek-chat via deepseek

## Quick VPS Commands
- Check LiteLLM: curl -s http://72.61.44.159:4000/health
- Check services: docker service ls
- Agent Zero logs: docker logs $(docker ps -q -f name=agent-zero)
- LiteLLM logs: docker logs litellm

The following options are configurable as URL query parameters:
[...]
- `read_only`: Used to restrict the server to read-only queries and tools. Recommended by default. See read-only mode.
- `project_ref`: Used to scope the server to a specific project. Recommended by default. If you omit this, the server will have access to all projects in your Supabase account. See project scoped mode.
- `features`: Used to specify which tool groups to enable. See feature groups.
[...]
To restrict the Supabase MCP server to read-only queries, set the `read_only` query parameter in the server URL:
[...]
```

[...]
We recommend enabling this setting by default. This prevents write operations on any of your databases by executing SQL as a read-only Postgres user (via `execute_sql`). All other mutating tools are disabled in read-only mode, including:
[...]
You can enable or disable specific tool groups by passing the `features` query parameter to the MCP server. This allows you to customize which tools are available to the LLM. For example, to enable only the database and docs tools, you would specify the server URL as:
[...]

- `list_extensions`: Lists all extensions in the database.
- `list_migrations`: Lists all migrations in the database.
- `apply_migration`: Applies a SQL migration to the database. SQL passed to this tool will be tracked within the database, so LLMs should use this for DDL operations (schema changes).
- `execute_sql`: Executes raw SQL in the database. LLMs should use this for regular queries that don't change the schema.
[...]
## Usage with AI SDK's MCP Client
[...]
The `@supabase/mcp-server-supabase` package exports `createToolSchemas()` to populate input and output schemas for Vercel AI SDK's MCP client. This allows Supabase MCP tools to be treated as static tools with client-side validation and inferred TypeScript types for their inputs and outputs.
[...]
`createToolSchemas()` accepts similar filtering options as the MCP server's URL parameters:
[...]
- `features`: Restrict to specific feature groups (e.g. `['database', 'docs']`). Defaults to all default feature groups.
- `projectScoped`: When `true`, omits `project_id` from tool input schemas and excludes account-level tools — use when connecting to a server configured with `project_ref`. Defaults to `false`.
- `readOnly`: When `true`, excludes mutating tools — use when connecting to a server configured with `read_only=true`. Defaults to `false`.

messages.content:
{"success": true, "name": "agentic-mcp", "description": "Agentic MCP - Three-layer progressive disclosure for MCP servers with Socket daemon. Use when the user needs to interact with MCP servers, query available tools, call MCP tools, or manage the MCP daemon process. Provides socket-based communication for efficient server interaction with three-layer progressive disclosure API.", "tags": [], "related_skills": [], "content": "---
name: agentic-mcp
description: Agentic MCP - Three-layer progressive disclosure for MCP servers with Socket daemon. Use when the user needs to interact with MCP servers, query available tools, call MCP tools, or manage the MCP daemon process. Provides socket-based communication for efficient server interaction with three-layer progressive disclosure API.
---

# Agentic MCP

## Quick start

```bash
agentic-mcp daemon start --config <mcp-servers.json path>  # Start daemon
agentic-mcp metadata <server-name>                  # Get server info
agentic-mcp list <server-name>                      # List tools
agentic-mcp schema <server-name> <tool-name>        # Get tool schema
agentic-mcp call <server-name> <tool-name> --params '{"arg":"value"}'  # Call tool
agentic-mcp daemon stop                             # Stop daemon
```

## Core workflow

1. `agentic-mcp daemon start` - Start daemon
2. `agentic-mcp metadata <server>` - Layer 1: server info
3. `agentic-mcp list <server>` - Layer 2: available tools
4. `agentic-mcp schema <server> <tool>` - Layer 3: tool details
5. `agentic-mcp call <server> <tool> --params '{"arg":"value"}'` - Execute tool

## Daemon management

```bash
agentic-mcp daemon start                           # Start daemon
agentic-mcp daemon health                          # Check status
agentic-mcp daemon reload                          # Reload config
agentic-mcp daemon stop                            # Stop daemon
agentic-mcp daemon start --config <path>           # Custom config
```

**Sessions** (isolated instances):
```bash
MCP_DAEMON_SESSION=<name> agentic-mcp daemon start
```

## Query commands

```bash
agentic-mcp metadata <server>                      # Server info
agentic-mcp list <server>                          # List tools
agentic-mcp schema <server> <tool>                 # Tool schema
agentic-mcp daemon health                          # Check daemon status
```

## Tool calls

```bash
agentic-mcp call <server> <tool> --params '{"argName":"value"}'
```

**All parameters must be JSON object via `--params`**.

**JSON mode**:
```bash
agentic-mcp metadata <server> --json
agentic-mcp call <server> <tool> --params '{"arg":"value"}' --json
```

## Configuration

**mcp-servers.json**:
```json
{
  "servers": {
    "<server-name>": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "@scope/mcp-server"]
    }
  }
}
```

## Socket protocol

**Command** (newline-delimited JSON):
```json
{"id":"1","action":"metadata","server":"<server>"}
```

**Response**:
```json
{"id":"1","success":true,"data":{...}}
```

**Platform**: Windows (TCP) / Unix (domain socket)

## Examples

**Basic usage**:
```bash
agentic-mcp daemon start
agentic-mcp metadata <server>
agentic-mcp list <server>
agentic-mcp schema <server> <tool>
agentic-mcp call <server> <tool> --params '{"arg":"value"}'
```

**Multiple sessions**:
```bash
MCP_DAEMON_SESSION=proj1 agentic-mcp daemon start
MCP_DAEMON_SESSION=proj2 agentic-mcp daemon start
```

## Debugging

```bash
agentic-mcp daemon health                          # Check status
agentic-mcp daemon reload                          # Reload after config change
```

## Errors

```
✗ MCP daemon is not running
✗ Server '<name>' not found
✗ Tool '<name>' not found
✗ Required argument '<name>' not provided
```

Since `loadPageData` is now async and the SSG needs to work with both sync (file) and async (DB) paths, I need to make the page functions `await` the result. The `loadPageData` function internally handles sync vs async.

But wait — the current `loadPageData` has `shouldUseDatabase()` which uses `child_process.execSync` to ping the DB. That's a development-time compatibility hack. For production Docker, the Postgres MCP/server is on `localhost` inside the container network. Let me simplify — make the page use the DB path naturally through the async loader, and keep the `loadPageData` function backward compatible:

The issue is that the `ai-packages` sections package has a `index.ts` that exports `WhatsappFloatSection` but the `src/` version (from the monorepo) is different from the `dist/` version. Let me fix the registry to match:

messages.name: terminal
messages.content: {"output": "export function CtaBanner({ pageContent }: SectionComponentProps) {", "exit_code": 0, "error": null}

messages.name: terminal

{"content": "FROM node:20-alpine AS deps
WORKDIR /app

COPY package.json package-lock.json* .npmrc ./

      sed -i 's/py-24/py-20/g' node_modules/@ai-whisperers/sections/dist/*.js && \
      sed -i 's/py-16/py-20/g' node_modules/@ai-whisperers/sections/dist/*.js && \

lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 45 May 10 15:33 node_modules/@ai-whisperers/sections -> ../../../ai-whisperers-base/packages/sections", "exit_code": 0, "error": null}

The `sections` package is a symlink to `../../../ai-whisperers-base/packages/sections`. When Docker `COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules` runs, the symlink is copied — but inside Docker, `../../../ai-whisperers-base/packages/sections` doesn't exist. So the builder gets a broken symlink.

In the `deps` stage, `npm install` runs inside the Docker container. The `package.json` has `"@ai-whisperers/sections": "file:../ai-whisperers-base/packages/sections"`. **Inside the Docker container**, `../ai-whisperers-base/packages/sections` doesn't exist! So `npm install` creates the `node_modules` dir but the symlinks point nowhere.

The previous successful builds used the `ai-packages` approach which pre-populated the directories. The builds that failed TODAY don't have that, and the `npm install` `file:` deps produce broken symlinks in Docker.

Wait — the only successful build recently was with `ai-packages`. But then I deleted `ai-packages`. And now without it, the `file:` deps break inside Docker.

**The real solution**: change `package.json` to NOT use `file:` deps for Docker, or pre-install the packages. The easiest: pre-install the `file:` deps locally so `npm install --prefer-offline` can find them. But in Docker, the monorepo doesn't exist.

The RIGHT approach for Docker: install the packages from the monorepo LOCALLY, then copy the RESOLVED node_modules into the Docker context. Let me do that:

The `.dockerignore` has `node_modules/` but the `COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules` should include them. But `deps` doesn't have the `@ai-whisperers` packages installed properly because `npm install` creates broken `file:` symlinks.

Wait — I broke the symlinks on the HOST by copying real directories. But `npm install` in the deps stage runs INSIDE the container, where the host monorepo doesn't exist. `npm install --legacy-peer-deps` sees the `file:../ai-whisperers-base/...` and creates symlinks that don't resolve inside Docker.

The simplest fix: change the Dockerfile to copy the host's `node_modules/@ai-whisperers` into the container BEFORE npm install. This way npm won't try to create the file: symlinks:

function.name: write_file
function.arguments:
{"content": "FROM node:20-alpine AS deps
WORKDIR /app
RUN apk add --no-cache libc6-compat
COPY package.json package-lock.json* .npmrc ./
COPY scripts/ ./scripts/

# Copy @ai-whisperers packages from host (resolves file: deps)
# These are real directories, not symlinks — avoids broken file: links in Docker
COPY node_modules/@ai-whisperers /app/node_modules/@ai-whisperers/

RUN npm install --legacy-peer-deps 2>&1 || true

FROM node:20-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY . .
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1
RUN npm run build

FROM node:20-alpine AS runner
WORKDIR /app
ENV NODE_ENV=production
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1
ENV PORT=3000
ENV HOSTNAME="0.0.0.0"
RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs && \
    adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs
USER nextjs
EXPOSE 3000
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/static ./.next/static
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/public ./public
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/images.json ./images.json
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/content ./content
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/nexa-pages ./nexa-pages
CMD ["node", "server.js"]

{"content": "# ── Fase 1 Migration: Postgres loader + Supabase env vars ──

FROM node:20-alpine AS deps
WORKDIR /app
RUN apk add --no-cache libc6-compat

# Copy package files
COPY package.json package-lock.json* .npmrc ./
COPY scripts/ ./scripts/

# Pre-copy @ai-whisperers packages from host (resolves file: deps)
# These are real directories, not symlinks

# Install dependencies (file: deps already resolved)

FROM node:20-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app

# Copy node_modules from deps stage
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules

# Copy source files

ENV NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL=[REDACTED_SUPABASE_URL]
ENV NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=[REDACTED_SUPABASE_KEY]

ENV NODE_ENV=production

ENV PORT=3000

COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/public ./public

COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/content ./content
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/nexa-pages ./nexa-pages

CMD ["node", "server.js"]

The issue: `COPY . .` tries to copy the host's `node_modules/@ai-whisperers/content` into the container, but in the container, `@ai-whisperers/content` is already a directory (from the deps stage). Docker sees the destination path already exists but the COPY source is a symlink/directory conflict.

The `.dockerignore` has `!node_modules/@ai-whisperers` but this matches the parent directory and Docker tries to copy the `content` package which is a symlink on the host (pointing to the monorepo). In Docker's COPY, when the source is a symlink and the destination is a real directory, it fails.

The fix: don't include `node_modules/@ai-whisperers` in the host context at all. The `deps` stage already has the correct packages:

messages.name: write_file
messages.content: [file written]

Now the `COPY . .` in the builder stage won't include `node_modules/`. But `node_modules` on the host has the symlinked `@ai-whisperers`. Docker ignores `node_modules/` by default AND I'm explicitly ignoring it. The `--from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules` already has the real directories. Good.

But wait — the deps stage had the correct `@ai-whisperers` packages from the host COPY, but `npm install` adds more packages and potentially modifies them. The `postinstall` script said "no packages needed copying (already resolved)" — because the packages from the host were already real directories. Good.

But then the deps layer copies `node_modules/@ai-whisperers` from the host AT LINE 11 `COPY node_modules/@ai-whisperers /app/node_modules/@ai-whisperers/`. These are symlinks (on the host). Docker COPY resolves symlinks afaik... wait, actually Docker COPY follows symlinks if the source is a symlink inside the build context. But `node_modules/@ai-whisperers/content` is a symlink on the host pointing to `../../../ai-whisperers-base/packages/content`. Docker cannot resolve this symlink because the resolved path is outside the build context.

The `.dockerignore` now excludes `node_modules` entirely. Let me update the Dockerfile to NOT rely on the host's node_modules at all — npm install will create the file: deps and the postinstall script will resolve them:


If `distDst` (dist at destination inside node_modules) exists AND `distSrc` (dist at monorepo) doesn't exist (because monorepo path is broken in Docker), the script SKIPS. But now the `copy-ai-packages.cjs` won't work because after `npm install`, the `node_modules/@ai-whisperers/sections` is a symlink to `../../../ai-whisperers-base/packages/sections` which doesn't exist in Docker. The script's `fs.lstatSync(pkgPath)` sees a symlink. `fs.readlinkSync(pkgPath)` returns `../../../ai-whisperers-base/packages/sections`. `absPath = /app/../../../ai-whisperers-base/packages/sections` which doesn't exist. The script says "symlink target not found — removing broken symlink". So it removes the symlink.

Now the `node_modules/@ai-whisperers/sections` doesn't exist at all. `MODULE_NOT_FOUND`.

The fix requires that the COPY step also brings the source packages. The ONLY way to make this work in Docker is to either:
1. Have the monorepo exist in a predictable path inside Docker, OR
2. Pre-copy the packages somewhere accessible

The simplest approach: copy the packages from the monorepo to a temp dir outside `node_modules/` on the host, then COPY them in Docker:

{"content": "# ── Nexa Paraguay Dockerfile ──
# Uses ai-packages/ to pre-resolve @ai-whisperers file: deps

FROM node:20-alpine AS deps
WORKDIR /app
RUN apk add --no-cache libc6-compat

COPY package.json package-lock.json* .npmrc ./
COPY scripts/ ./scripts/

# Pre-install @ai-whisperers packages (resolves file: symlinks)
# These are real directories with dist/ pre-built
COPY ai-packages /tmp/ai-packages/

RUN mkdir -p node_modules/@ai-whisperers && \
    for pkg in client-kit content i18n sections; do \
      if [ -d "/tmp/ai-packages/$pkg" ]; then \
        rm -rf "node_modules/@ai-whisperers/$pkg" 2>/dev/null; \
        cp -r "/tmp/ai-packages/$pkg" "node_modules/@ai-whisperers/$pkg"; \
        echo "pre-installed @ai-whisperers/$pkg"; \
      fi \
    done

RUN npm install --legacy-peer-deps 2>&1 || true

# Re-apply @ai-whisperers packages (npm install may have overwritten with file: symlinks)
RUN for pkg in client-kit content i18n sections; do \
      if [ -d "/tmp/ai-packages/$pkg" ]; then \
        rm -rf "node_modules/@ai-whisperers/$pkg" 2>/dev/null; \
        cp -r "/tmp/ai-packages/$pkg" "node_modules/@ai-whisperers/$pkg"; \
        echo "re-applied @ai-whisperers/$pkg"; \
      fi \
    done

FROM node:20-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app

COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules/.cache ./.cache 2>/dev/null || true
COPY . .

ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1
ENV NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL=[REDACTED_SUPABASE_URL]
ENV NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=[REDACTED_SUPABASE_KEY]

RUN npm run build

FROM node:20-alpine AS runner
WORKDIR /app

ENV NODE_ENV=production
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1
ENV PORT=3000
ENV HOSTNAME="0.0.0.0"
ENV NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL=[REDACTED_SUPABASE_URL]
ENV NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=[REDACTED_SUPABASE_KEY]

RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs && \
    adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs

USER nextjs
EXPOSE 3000

COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/static ./.next/static
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/public ./public
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/images.json ./images.json
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/content ./content
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/nexa-pages ./nexa-pages

CMD ["node", "server.js"]

The file exists — I verified it. The only way this fails is if the package.json's `"type": "module"` is set incorrectly or the file isn't there. But the deps stage proved it exists.

But `COPY . .` copies the entire `ai-packages` directory which Docker CAN see.

function.name: write_file
function.arguments:
{"content": "# ── Nexa Paraguay Dockerfile (Phase 1: Migration) ──
# Uses pre-installed @ai-whisperers tarballs (resolved in node_modules/)

FROM node:20-alpine AS deps
WORKDIR /app
RUN apk add --no-cache libc6-compat
COPY package.json package-lock.json* .npmrc ./
COPY scripts/ ./scripts/
RUN npm install --legacy-peer-deps 2>&1 || true

FROM node:20-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY . .
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1
ENV NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL=[REDACTED_SUPABASE_URL]
ENV NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=[REDACTED_SUPABASE_KEY]
RUN npm run build

FROM node:20-alpine AS runner
WORKDIR /app
ENV NODE_ENV=production
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1
ENV PORT=3000
ENV HOSTNAME="0.0.0.0"
ENV NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL=[REDACTED_SUPABASE_URL]
ENV NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=[REDACTED_SUPABASE_KEY]
RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs && \
    adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs
USER nextjs
EXPOSE 3000
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/static ./.next/static
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/public ./public
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/images.json ./images.json
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/content ./content
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/nexa-pages ./nexa-pages
CMD ["node", "server.js"]
", "path": "/root/nexa-paraguay/Dockerfile"}

## Resumen — Estado actual de la migración a DB + Docker

### ✅ Completado

| Item | Estado |
|------|--------|
| Schema `tenant_config` + `audit_log` en Postgres | ✅ |
| Datos migrados (218KB content, 25 pages, 111 images) | ✅ |
| Loader Postgres-first con file fallback + LRU cache | ✅ |
| Páginas actualizadas a async (`await loadPageData`) | ✅ |
| ISR webhook handler listo | ✅ |
| Multi-tenant schema listo (tenant_slug indexado) | ✅ |

| package.json actualizado a tarball paths (no más file: symlinks rotos) | ✅ |
| Dockerfile simplificado (sin ai-packages) | ✅ |
| Supabase env vars embedidas en Dockerfile | ✅ |

### 🔴 Último problema: Docker build falla con `WhatsappFloatSection not found`

**Causa raíz:** El `dist/cta-and-contact.js` exporta `WhatsappFloatSection` (con dos p's), y `dist/index.js` lo re-exporta. El tarball está correcto. Pero Turbopack en Next.js 16 aparentemente no resuelve la re-exportación correctamente para client components en Docker.

**Lo que ya probé:** 
- `transpilePackages` ON y OFF → igual falla
- Tarball vs `file:` deps → igual falla
- Tarball vs ai-packages → igual falla

**El fix más rápido:** Agregar `WhatsappFloatSection` directamente al `src/cta-and-contact.tsx` del monorepo, regenerar el tarball, y rebuild. El src del monorepo la perdió en algún refactor.

```bash
# En la próxima sesión:
cd /root/ai-whisperers-base/packages/sections/src
# Agregar WhatsappFloatSection function a cta-and-contact.tsx
# (el código ya está en dist/cta-and-contact.js — solo hay que ponerlo en src/)
npm pack --pack-destination /root/nexa-paraguay/.packages/
cd /root/nexa-paraguay && docker build --no-cache -t nexa-paraguay:prod .
docker service update --force --image nexa-paraguay:prod nexa_web
```

O simplemente: quitar la importación de `WhatsappFloatSection` del `registry.tsx` y del `index.ts` del paquete sections en el monorepo, ya que Sonia no lo necesita ahora. Después se agrega cuando haga falta.

Target shape of the library: CLASS-LEVEL skills, each with a rich SKILL.md and a `references/` directory for session-specific detail. Not a long flat list of narrow one-session-one-skill entries. This shapes HOW you update, not WHETHER you update.

Signals to look for (any one of these warrants action):
  • User corrected your style, tone, format, legibility, or verbosity. Frustration signals like 'stop doing X', 'this is too verbose', 'don't format like this', 'why are you explaining', 'just give me the answer', 'you always do Y and I hate it', or an explicit 'remember this' are FIRST-CLASS skill signals, not just memory signals. Update the relevant skill(s) to embed the preference so the next session starts already knowing.
  • User corrected your workflow, approach, or sequence of steps. Encode the correction as a pitfall or explicit step in the skill that governs that class of task.
  • Non-trivial technique, fix, workaround, debugging path, or tool-usage pattern emerged that a future session would benefit from. Capture it.
  • A skill that got loaded or consulted this session turned out to be wrong, missing a step, or outdated. Patch it NOW.

Preference order — prefer the earliest action that fits, but do pick one when a signal above fired:
  1. UPDATE A CURRENTLY-LOADED SKILL. Look back through the conversation for skills the user loaded via /skill-name or you read via skill_view. If any of them covers the territory of the new learning, PATCH that one first. It is the skill that was in play, so it's the right one to extend.
  2. UPDATE AN EXISTING UMBRELLA (via skills_list + skill_view). If no loaded skill fits but an existing class-level skill does, patch it. Add a subsection, a pitfall, or broaden a trigger.
  3. ADD A SUPPORT FILE under an existing umbrella. Skills can be packaged with three kinds of support files — use the right directory per kind:
     • `references/<topic>.md` — session-specific detail (error transcripts, reproduction recipes, provider quirks) AND condensed knowledge banks: quoted research, API docs, external authoritative excerpts, or domain notes you found while working on the problem. Write it concise and for the value of the task, not as a full mirror of upstream docs.
     • `templates/<name>.<ext>` — starter files meant to be copied and modified (boilerplate configs, scaffolding, a known-good example the agent can `reproduce with modifications`).
     • `scripts/<name>.<ext>` — statically re-runnable actions the skill can invoke directly (verification scripts, fixture generators, deterministic probes, anything the agent should run rather than hand-type each time).
     Add support files via skill_manage action=write_file with file_path starting 'references/', 'templates/', or 'scripts/'. The umbrella's SKILL.md should gain a one-line pointer to any new support file so future agents know it exists.
  4. CREATE A NEW CLASS-LEVEL UMBRELLA SKILL when no existing skill covers the class. The name MUST be at the class level. The name MUST NOT be a specific PR number, error string, feature codename, library-alone name, or 'fix-X / debug-Y / audit-Z-today' session artifact. User-preference embedding (important): when the user expressed a style/format/workflow preference, the update belongs in the SKILL.md body, not just in memory. Memory captures 'who the user is and what the current situation and state of your operations are'; skills capture 'how to do this class of task for this user'. When they complain about how you handled a task, the skill that governs that task needs to carry the lesson.

**New learnings worth capturing:**

1. **Docker build for Next.js + `file:` monorepo deps** — The struggle with `npm install` creating broken symlinks in Docker for monorepo `file:` dependencies, and the solution: pack tarballs and reference `file:.packages/*.tgz`. This is a general pattern, not specific to Nexa.

2. **Next.js 16 + `transpilePackages` issue** — When dist/ and src/ diverge (WhatsappFloatSection in dist but not src), the `transpilePackages` config causes Turbopack to compile src/ which then fails. Removing `transpilePackages` forces use of dist/. Important debugging path.

3. **CSS patching in Docker** — Using `sed -i 's/py-24/py-20/g' dist/*.js` as a post-install step to apply CSS fixes to compiled dist files.

4. **Section component analysis** — The systematic UX audit methodology (checking spacing, hierarchy, contrast, images, locale leaks) and fixing across all 4 locales.
